11 min read
0:00 / 10:52
We need some basic infrastructure to validate ideas —
especially in the early stages when we’re making our �rst few
turns of the TDB �ywheel.
At the most basic level, we need a way to generate awareness
for our ideas, some kind of landing page ‘container’ that
expresses those ideas, a next action that creates a signal (e.g.,
opt-in, scheduled call, sale, etc.), and tools to measure
performance.
We’ll use Facebook ads to generate awareness (that’s the
subject of Validate, Part IV). However, before we do that, we’ll
need to set up the minimum viable infrastructure required to
get the feedback we need to iterate our way to success.
Broadly speaking, there are two options we can implement.
Option 1 is to use a hosted solution that meets some or all of
our initial needs. Those options include landing page builders
like Leadpages and Unbounce, or more robust solutions like
ClickFunnels (that also includes email, ecommerce, and
content-creation / sharing capabilities).
Build, Part I (Minimum Viable
Infrastructure)
MP3 File 3.73 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

Option 2 is to build a solution using more robust tools like
WordPress, integrating other speci�c solutions like email
service providers (ConvertKit, for example) and commerce
(ClickBank, for example).
There is no right answer here — each has positives and
negatives.
In general, option 1 is faster in the short term. It’s very easy to
set up a lead-gen page in Unbounce, for example, or an opt-in
page with email follow-up in Clickfunnels.
However, one signi�cant danger with option 1 is the
opportunity cost associated with spinning up temporary
solutions that have to be recreated later as your business
matures.
That leads us to the primary bene�t of option 2, which is it’s a
more durable solution. There’s more work to set up in the
beginning but less work overall because you won’t have to
migrate content, leads, etc.
Another potential issue with option 1 is that the temporary
solution becomes the long-term solution. It’s dif�cult to break
free from the inertia of a working solution (and often, that’s
most dif�cult when your business is growing rapidly).
We’ll explain the options in more detail below and share the
approach we’ll be taking.
Please remember that whatever option you choose should be
in service to the larger goal of validating your initial
ideas — and whatever you create should be the minimum
necessary to accomplish that task.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

There will be many opportunities to expand and improve
your infrastructure over time in future turns of the TDB
�ywheel.
In the startup world, the term MVP (minimum viable product) is
used to represent a minimum version of an idea or product to
collect the maximum amount of validated learning about
customers with the least risk (as measured by time, energy, and
dollars).
However, we’re not there yet. We’re still seeking to validate
(iterate towards) a working offer (a marketing expression of a
product or service that has a message-to-market match).
For this reason, we prefer to use a different term to describe this
part of the process. To this end, a minimum viable experiment
(MVE) more closely expresses our intent.
An MVE will inform a downstream MVP (our Release 1.0 product).
In this lesson, we’ll build our MVE environment.
Infrastructure: Landing Page /
Website
This section can be tricky because it has the potential to open
Pandora’s box. This can be fertile ground to procrastinate. Don’t do
that.
The goal is to build whatever is minimally necessary to get your
initial ideas out into the world and get feedback from your
prospective audience.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

To do that, you can use off-the-shelf page or site builders (option 1
mentioned above), or set up a more robust site development
environment and then build only the infrastructure you need for
your experiments (option 2).
Let’s start by looking at Option 1: page / site builders.
If you’re already paying for one (or more) hosted page or site-
building solutions, we recommended that you use those to
minimize friction to get started.
Pricing can vary from a few bucks (Wix) to hundreds of dollars
each month, depending on your needs. Some popular solutions
include:
▪ Unbounce
▪ Leadpages
▪ Wix
▪ Instapage
▪ Clickfunnels
▪ HubSpot
▪ Simplero
(If you use ConvertKit, it comes with a free landing page builder.)
The second option is a more robust website-builder (like
WordPress).
If you don’t already use a landing page or site-building service,
our suggestion, broadly speaking, is to consider what you’ll likely
use long-term for your business.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

We’re not a fan building a business on someone else’s platform
(for example, Clickfunnels). Although it’s relatively quick to get set
up if money isn’t a constraint, there are many less-obvious
constraints downstream if you were to “run” your business on a
marketing platform like Clickfunnels or HubSpot.
We prefer owning our own platform, so we’re not beholden to a
gatekeeper or subject to the limitations of the platform we’re
using (for example, site speed issues mentioned below).
That said, it’s easy to get caught up researching the perfect self-
hosted tech stack and then never make it out the gate.
For this reason, we’re going to give you our lean self-hosted stack,
with the minimum number of options to get started. This is the
exact stack we use ourselves. From there, you can orient yourself
to options that better match your needs and budget.
We use WordPress because it’s durable, extensible, and it powers a
quarter of the world’s websites. There’s an enormous developer
community for WordPress, and a rich ecosystem of add-ons and
integrations that make it easier to use and more powerful.
Our self-hosted lean tech stack includes:
▪ WordPress (direct download) — preinstalled on our
recommended hosting platforms
▪ Hosting (we use Kinsta and WP Engine) because they’re
reliable and fast
▪ (Kinsta starts at $30/mo and WPE at $25/mo and both
are best in class1 )
▪ Premium themes (Thesis/Focus or GeneratePress)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

▪ (Thesis/Focus: $87/year for a single site license, or
$187/year for �ve sites 2 )
That’s less than $40/mo for a best-in-class self-hosted solution.
One of the nice things with both Kinsta & WP Engine is that
deploying a site is one-click easy (WP deployed, optimized, with
SSL/HTTPS included).
If you want to “go big” (�ve sites), you’re looking at around
$115/mo. That’s incredible value.
1 Note about hosting: We can say without a shred of hesitation —
having cumulatively been paying for hosting for near enough forty
years — that you get what you pay for. Yes, you can �nd unlimited
website hosting for �ve bucks a month, but we caution against that.
It’s one thing hosting a personal blog (yes, cheap hosting will do just
�ne), but orders of magnitude di�erent when speed and uptime
matter. When you’re paying for tra�c, the last thing you want to
discover hours later is that your host was down or sluggish when it
mattered most.
In stark contrast, both Kinsta and WPE run on Google Cloud
Platform, a globally-distributed network of data centers, running (C2)
virtual machines, each with up to 60 CPUs and hundreds of gigabytes
of RAM.
Put another way, TLB runs on the same servers and hardware stack as
Google.com, and for as little as $30/month. It never goes down, and
it’s always ridiculously fast.
2Note about WordPress themes: It’s tempting to seek out a free
WordPress theme that can build decent-looking landing pages to keep
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

costs low.
It’s even more tempting to use a Page Builder like Divi or Elementor, to
essentially have your own self-hosted version of Clickfunnels or
Leadpages, where building landing pages is drag-and-drop easy…
This is a decision only you can make, but we would be doing you a
disservice if we didn’t draw attention to the alternative. Then,
whatever decision you make is an informed one.
Divi and Elementor on WordPress are not lightweight a�airs (the
same can be said for hosted solutions like Clickfunnels and
Leadpages). Technically speaking, they’re dogs. The HTML code they
produce to create landing pages is bloated (1000s of lines of code when
100s would do).
The homepage of Clickfunnels is 3437 lines long (the rendered version
is not a long page). The homepage of bu�er.com (rendered version
looks similar in length to CF) is 356 lines. That’s 10x smaller, which
means it can load that much faster.
Speed matters.
This is why we’ve been using Thesis/Focus for well over a decade
(Focus is a new skin that runs on top of the Thesis framework). It’s
(very!) lightweight, which means it’s fast!
Thesis/Focus is what we use on TLB (and the demo site we built for
you). If we couldn’t use Thesis/Focus for whatever reason,
GeneratePress would be our second choice. It’s also lightweight and
produces well-optimized pages. Unlike with Thesis/Focus, GP has a
free version, and the Premium version, which unlocks the good stu�,
runs at just $59/year for a license.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

Our recommendation, if it’s not already obvious, if you decide to go
with a self-hosted WordPress platform, use Thesis/Focus (our #1
choice) or GeneratePress.
That’s it.
To help you visualize what a landing page can look like, we’ve
built a demo version you can use to inspire your own. It’s not
meant to be a “template” for you to copy. We’re not going to give
you our custom CSS. This is intended to help give you an idea of
how we build landing pages. There are a million variations, none
better than the next within a given context.
We’ll keep tweaking and adding to this demo site (we’ll create
different “scenarios” within it throughout the training). Please
don’t link to the site from your blog (if you have one) or the
socials. It’s exclusively for TDB students. We would appreciate it if
it remained “within the family,” so to speak.
https://thedurablebusiness.xyz
Infrastructure: Tracking
To measure results, we suggest that you set up and install:
▪ Google Tag Manager (optional, but highly recommended)
▪ Google Analytics
▪ Facebook Pixel
If you choose option 2, there are third-party tools to easily deploy
Google Tag Manager in a WordPress environment. A quick
YouTube or Google search will show you how to install GTM
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 9 --

manually as well.
If you’re using a page or site-building tool, consult that speci�c
tool’s support documentation. Some tools don’t work well with
Google Tag Manager, for example, and most have a custom
method for installing code appropriately. A quick Google search
should reveal details for whatever platforms you’re choosing.
Make sure your tracking tools are set up and tested before you
start your �rst experiment. You don’t want to �nd out tracking
was setup incorrectly after the fact.
Finally, here’s the high-level work�ow you’ll want to follow to
prepare to run your �rst experiment:
�. Validate, Part II (Messaging & Writing Framework)
�. Validate, Part III (Testing Framework)
�. Build, Part I (Landing Page Framework)
�. Validate, Part IV (FB Testing Traf�c Framework)
Ask questions in the comments below and discuss in the
community.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
